did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a modified U-net deep learning network for the automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions. The method was evaluated on the PH2 and DermIS datasets, achieving Dice coefficients of 0.933 and 0.872 respectively.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a modified U-net deep learning approach for melanoma segmentation. It details specific methods (post-processing techniques), datasets used (PH2 and DermIS), and reports new quantitative results (Dice coefficients of 0.933 and 0.872) compared against related works. Although published in a conference proceedings (HEALTHCOM), it contains original research results rather than being a review or survey.

title: Automatic Segmentation of Melanoma Skin Cancer Using Deep Learning
journal: 2020 IEEE International Conference on E-health Networking, Application & Services (HEALTHCOM)
brief_summary: This study proposes a modified U-net deep learning network combined with post-processing techniques for the automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions. The method was evaluated on two public datasets, PH2 and DermIS, to address the complexity of segmenting lesions with abnormal shapes and artifacts. The primary evaluation metric used was the Dice coefficient, alongside sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy. The proposed approach achieved an average Dice coefficient of 0.933 on the PH2 dataset and 0.872 on the DermIS dataset. These results demonstrate that the modified U-net is highly effective and surpasses several existing methods in the literature. The study concludes that the technique is promising for assisting doctors in early melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for segmentation. The paper focuses on automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions. In the DermIS dataset, 119 images were identified as melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.role: Comparator class / Exclusion from primary focus
lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned in the context of the DermIS dataset composition (88 non-melanoma images) and general skin cancer types (BCC, SCC) in the introduction, but the segmentation task focused on melanoma. The paper notes that non-melanoma class characteristics are often too adjusted for melanoma segmentation.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a main type of skin cancer caused by UV radiation, but not a target class for the segmentation experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a modified U-net deep learning network for the automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions. The model predicts pixel-wise masks for the lesion region. Evaluation metrics include Dice coefficient (DSC), Jaccard index (JAC), Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy, which are standard for segmentation tasks. The primary results reported are Dice coefficients of 0.933 (PH2 dataset) and 0.872 (DermIS dataset).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dermatology dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions with expert segmentation masks. It is one of the two public datasets used in the study.

use.training: Used for training the modified U-net model via 5-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: DermIS (Dermatology Information System)
datasets_used.description: A public dermatology information service providing elaborate and complete image atlases of skin diseases. The study gathered 207 images from this database, including diagnoses and case reports.

use.training: Used for training the modified U-net model via 5-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the total dataset into mutually exclusive subsets for training and testing, increasing the reliability of the results by using different combinations of data.
To report the variability of the results obtained from the cross-validation cycles alongside the mean metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and sample standard deviation) from 5-fold cross-validation and compared qualitatively with related works in the literature.

architecture_details.structure: Contraction path to capture contextual information and symmetrical expansion path for precise segmentation
architecture_details.subsampling: Four subsampling layers with medium activation function
architecture_details.final_layer: 1x1 convolution followed by sigmoid activation function
architecture_details.modification: Differs from original Ronneberger et al. architecture in the number of layers

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: U-net within a Generative Adversarial Network
Fully automated deep learning ensemble method
preprocessing_techniques: Proportional resizing of images to 128x128 and 256x256 pixels
Color space conversion to RGB and Grayscale (GRAY)
Post-processing morphological closing (dilation followed by erosion with a disc structural element of size 5)
Post-processing region mapping to select the largest area as the segmentation mask
Post-processing hole filling using binary expansions
conclusions: The proposed modified U-net deep learning network combined with post-processing techniques proved to be highly effective for the automatic segmentation of melanoma skin lesions, even when using small datasets.
The method achieved an average Dice coefficient of 0.933 on the PH2 dataset and 0.872 on the DermIS dataset, demonstrating high performance.

Post-processing steps (disconnecting extra regions, removing loose regions, and filling holes) generally improved segmentation results, particularly increasing Dice and Jaccard metrics, although some reductions in sensitivity and AUC were observed in the DermIS dataset due to errors in selecting the largest region.

The proposed method surpassed several existing methods in the literature in terms of Dice coefficient and Jaccard index on the PH2 dataset, and showed improved accuracy and Dice coefficient compared to relevant works on the DermIS dataset.

The study suggests that while the technique is promising and efficient for assisting doctors in early melanoma diagnosis, future work should evaluate the impact of preprocessing techniques (such as hair removal and noise reduction) and assess whether the network learns lesion-specific features or dataset-specific features through cross-testing between different datasets.
important_remarks: The study relies on two small public datasets (PH2 with 200 images and DermIS with 207 images), which limits the generalizability and robustness of the deep learning model.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values or significance tests); comparisons with related works are based on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) from 5-fold cross-validation.

Post-processing techniques (morphological closing, region mapping, hole filling) improved Dice and Jaccard scores but caused reductions in sensitivity and AUC on the DermIS dataset due to errors in selecting the largest region.
The model was trained using Google Collaboratory, a cloud-based platform, which raises potential concerns regarding data privacy and the reproducibility of hardware-specific training conditions.
The authors acknowledge the need for future cross-testing between different datasets to determine if the network learns lesion-specific features or dataset-specific features.

The DermIS dataset used in this study is a subset of the broader DermIS service, containing only 207 images (119 melanoma, 88 non-melanoma), which may not represent the full diversity of the source database.

The paper compares results with related works that used different numbers of images or different subsets of the DermIS dataset (e.g., [29] used only two images), making direct quantitative comparisons potentially biased or invalid.
